content.full_text:
SUPERSEDED
by: 96-103(On/out)

California
Fair Political
Practices
Commission

April 14, 1994

Robert M. Hertzberg
13022 Riverside Drive, #8
Sherman Oaks, CA 91403

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-94-082

Dear Mr. Hertzberg:

This is in response to your letter of March 3, 1994, in which
you seek advice regarding your duties in regards to the
expenditure of campaign funds under the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").1 and confirmation of telephone advice provided to your
deputy campaign manager, Barbara Creme, on February 9, 1994 by
Deanne Stone and on March 3, 1994 by Jeanette Turvill. Since your
questions are general in nature, we are treating your request as
one for informal assistance.2

QUESTIONS

1. You have chosen not to pursue your candidacy for the
Democratic nomination for the 40th Assembly District this year.
Because you neither left an elected office nor were defeated for
elected office, are your campaign funds considered surplus funds
under the Act?

2. If the answer to Question 1 is in the affirmative, may
you use your own discretion in returning campaign contributions
and allocating expenses?

3. Is it legally permissible for you to use campaign funds
to return contributions to your contributors on something other
than a pro rata basis?

_________________________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
Regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-94-082
Page 2


4. Is it legally permissible for you to use campaign funds
to purchase gifts for several of your volunteers, consultants, and
others to express your gratitude for their assistance in your
campaign effort?

CONCLUSIONS

1. The campaign funds of a candidate who has not left or
been defeated for elected office are not considered surplus funds
under the Act.

2. Even if campaign funds are not considered to be surplus
funds, they may not be expended solely at the candidate's
discretion. The Act requires that at a minimum, a candidate's
expenditure of campaign funds be reasonably related to a
political, legislative, or governmental purpose. If the
expenditure confers a substantial personal benefit on the
candidate, the expenditure must be directly related to a
political, legislative, or governmental purpose. A "substantial
personal benefit" is defined as a direct personal benefit to the
candidate of $100 or more.

3. Whether it is appropriate for a candidate to return
contributions only to selected contributors must be analyzed on a
case-by-case basis. The candidate must demonstrate that something
other than a pro rata basis return of contributions is reasonably
related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose.
From the facts you have provided, we find that you have satisfied
this criteria.

4. So long as the recipients of the gifts are employees or
workers of your campaign committee and so long as the cost of
gifts to each employee or campaign worker, when aggregated with
all other gifts in the calendar year is less than $250, it is
permissible for you to use campaign funds to buy them gifts.

FACTS

For a number of reasons, you have chosen not to pursue your
candidacy for the Democratic nomination for the 40th Assembly
District. In the meantime, however, you have raised and spent
money and are now seeking to wind up and terminate the Hertzberg
Committee for 40th Assembly District.

Barbara Creme, your deputy campaign manager, spoke with
Deanne Stone on February 9, 1994, and with Jeanette Turvill on
March 3, 1994, with respect to the inquiries above. It is your
understanding that because you have not resigned from a political
position or been defeated for an elective office, your campaign
funds are not governed by the statutory provision which requires
the pro rata return of surplus campaign funds. By this, you

Our File No. I-94-082
Page 3


assume that it is legally permissible for you to use your own
discretion in returning campaign funds and allocating expenses.
Specifically, you can assume you can decide which contributors should
be reimbursed fully, partially, or not at all.

In addition, it is your understanding that the Act places a
$250 limit on using campaign funds to buy gifts for volunteers,
consultants, and others who assisted you in your campaign effort.
You assume that it is legally permissible to use campaign funds to
purchase personal gifts for these individuals as long as the gifts
are within the $250 dollar limit.

ANALYSIS

Our records reflect that Ms. Creme was informed that when a
candidate decides not to pursue his or her candidacy after raising
funds for this purpose, the statutory provision governing surplus
funds does not apply. You are correct in stating the
applicable statute, Section 89519, requires that contributions, if
returned to contributors, must be done on a pro rata basis.
However, our records also reflect that no inquiry was made if that
meant it was legally permissible for a candidate to return
contributions to contributors on a selective basis.

In addressing this question, we look to Sections 89510 and
89512 of the Act, since no specific rules govern the return of
contributions from campaign funds that are not considered surplus
funds. Section 89510(b) states:

All contributions deposited into the campaign
account shall be deemed to be held in trust for
expenses associated with the election of the
candidate to the specific office...that he or she
intends to seek....

Section 89512 states the general rule of the personal
use law:

An expenditure to seek office is within the lawful
execution of the trust imposed by Section 89510 if
it is reasonably related to a political purpose....

(Emphasis added.)

Where an expenditure confers a substantial personal benefit³
on a candidate, the expenditure must be directly related to a
political, legislative, or governmental purpose. For this reason,


3
A substantial personal benefit is defined as a direct personal
benefit to the candidate or officeholder of $100 or more.
(Section 89511(b)(3).)

Our File No. I-94-082
Page 4


Section 89513(f)(1) prohibits the use of campaign funds to make
personal gifts unless the gift is directly related to a political,
legislative, or governmental purpose. However, this provision
goes on to state that "[t]he refund of a campaign contribution
does not constitute the making of a gift."

Based on a reading of Sections 89510, 89512, and 89513, it is
clear that campaign funds may be returned to contributors so long
as the return is reasonably related to a political purpose. (Peri
Advice Letter, No. A-93-183.) In the Peri Advice Letter, we
addressed a comparable situation. Senator Hart had decided not to
run for the Office of Superintendent of Public Instruction prior
to a fundraising event for that elected office but after the
tickets had been sold. We advised him that it was legally
permissible for him to return the money to the people who had
purchased tickets to the fundraiser. The return of these monies,
i.e., contributions, was considered reasonably related to a
political purpose because it assisted in maintaining the
candidate's credibility with his contributors.

On the other hand, we advised a controlled local ballot
measure committee that the refund of contributions to one or more
select contributors, because they were major contributors, did not
possess the requisite political, legislative, or governmental
purpose. (Hiltachk Advice Letter, No. I-90-053.)

Thus, whether it is appropriate for a candidate to return
contributions only to selected contributors must be analyzed on a
case-by-case basis. (Hiltachk Advice Letter, supra.)

On April 12, 1994, you notified this office that it was your
intention to pursue your candidacy for the same Assembly seat
(40th District), however, in 1996. You are in the process of
personally contacting each of the contributors to your campaign to
see if, in view of these new circumstances, the contributor would
object to your campaign committee retaining his or her
contribution. If each contributor asked for a return of his or
her contribution because you had decided not to pursue your
candidacy at this time, depending on the availability of campaign
funds, you would return the contributions to the contributors on a
pro rata basis.

We conclude, on the basis of these facts, that the return of
contributions to contributors to your campaign on other than a pro
rata basis is reasonably related to a political purpose for the
same reasons as stated to Senator Hart in the Peri Advice Letter,
supra.

You have also asked whether you may use campaign funds to buy
gifts for several volunteers, consultants, and others to express

Our File No. I-94-082
Page 5


your gratitude for their assistance in your campaign effort. You
understand that such gifts would be limited to $250.

     As indicated above, the Act provides that campaign funds may
not be used to make personal gifts unless the gift is directly
related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose.
(Section 89513(f)). Specifically, Section 89513(f)(2) provides:

          An election victory celebration or similar
     campaign event, or gifts with a total cumulative
     value of less than two hundred fifty dollars ($250),
     in a single year made to an individual employee, a
     campaign committee worker, or an employee of the
     elected officer's agency, are considered to be
     directly related to a political, legislative, or
     governmental purpose. For purposes of this
     paragraph, a gift to a member of a person's
     immediate family shall be deemed to be a gift to
     that person.

                    (Emphasis added.)

     Thus, so long as the recipients of the gifts are employees or
workers of your campaign committee⁴ and so long as the cost of
gifts to each employee or campaign worker, when aggregated with
all other gifts in the calendar year,⁵ is less than $250, you may
use your campaign funds for this purpose.⁶


_________________________

4    In your letter, in addition to "volunteers" and "consultants,"
you include a category called "others." In order for you to use
campaign funds to make a gift to persons other than volunteers,
employees, or workers of your campaign committee, you would have
to demonstrate that a gift to such a recipient is directly related
to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose.

5    Any other gifts to an employee or campaign worker during the
year would reduce the amount that can be expended on the gift

6    The Act provides that if a gift is less than $250 and the
recipient of the gift is a volunteer, employee, or worker of your
campaign committee, the gift is presumed to be directly related to
a political, legislative, or governmental purpose. This is not to
say that gifts from campaign funds made to any person which are
directly related to a political, legislative, or governmental
purpose are limited by the personal use law. However, other areas
of the Act may subject the recipient to gift limits. (See Section
89500 et. seq., the Ethics in Government Act of 1990.)

Our File No. I-94-082
Page 6


     If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.7

                              Sincerely,

                              Steven G. Churchwell
                              General Counsel

                              [signature: Deanne Stone]
                              By: Deanne Stone
                              Senior Commission Counsel




_____________________________
7 Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.
